questions.question_text: What is the fundamental premise of the Turing Test?

options.A: A computer must convince a judge it can think like a human
options.B: A computer must demonstrate consciousness through physical actions
options.C: A computer must be indistinguishable from a human in conversation
options.D: A computer must solve problems faster than a human

questions.misconception_description: Students confuse the Turing Test's actual criterion (behavioral indistinguishability in conversation) with requiring the computer to genuinely 'think.' The test is about external behavior, not internal mental states or consciousness.

questions.question_text: What does the Stroop Effect demonstrate about reading?

options.A: That reading requires conscious effort and attention
options.B: That color naming is faster than word reading
options.C: That reading can occur automatically and interfere with other tasks
options.D: That people cannot read and name colors simultaneously

questions.misconception_description: Students misinterpret the Stroop Effect as showing complete inability to perform both tasks, when it actually demonstrates interference from automatic processing. People can name colors, but automatic word reading slows them down—it's interference, not impossibility.

questions.question_text: Why can't you see your own eye making a saccade when looking in a mirror?

options.A: Your brain suppresses visual input during the rapid eye movement
options.B: Saccades occur too quickly for conscious perception
options.D: Your eyes close briefly during saccades

questions.misconception_description: Students attribute saccadic suppression to simple speed or timing limitations, when it's actually an active neural mechanism. The brain actively suppresses visual processing during saccades to prevent blurred images—it's not just that the movement is 'too fast to see.'

questions.question_text: According to John Searle (1990), who came up with the Chinese Room argument, which of the following would describe a computer that successfully passes the Turing test?
questions.misconception_description: Students conflate passing the Turing Test with genuine intelligence or understanding. Searle's argument explicitly distinguishes weak AI (simulation of intelligence without understanding) from strong AI (genuine understanding). Passing the Turing Test demonstrates weak AI—behavioral simulation, not true comprehension.
